startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: Work through this clinical case to test your understanding of key concepts in Integrated Physiology. Make diagnostic and management decisions based on the clinical scenario.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You made the correct clinical decisions at each step. You demonstrated strong understanding of the underlying concepts. Well done!
You showed some understanding but chose a suboptimal path. Review the feedback provided and try again to reinforce your learning.
Your clinical reasoning needs strengthening in this area. Review the source material carefully, paying attention to the key concepts highlighted, then attempt the case again.

params.text:
Case: Mountain Trekker

A 30-year-old trekker ascends rapidly to 4,500 metres altitude. Within 24 hours, he develops headache, nausea, and breathlessness. His SpO2 is 78%. His heart rate is 110 bpm. A blood sample shows haemoglobin of 14 g/dL (baseline was 13 g/dL).

Key concept: Acclimatisation to high altitude involves increased erythropoietin production leading to polycythaemia. The body compensates for hypoxia through multiple integrated mechanisms.

branchingQuestion.question: What is the body's primary long-term compensatory mechanism for high-altitude hypoxia?

alternatives.text: Increased erythropoietin production leading to polycythaemia (more red blood cells)

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: EPO production increases in response to hypoxia, stimulating erythropoiesis and eventually causing polycythaemia — the hallmark of altitude acclimatisation.

alternatives.text: Immediate increase in haemoglobin concentration through haemoconcentration only

feedback.title: Incomplete
feedback.subtitle: Haemoconcentration may occur acutely, but the key long-term adaptation is increased EPO-driven erythropoiesis leading to true polycythaemia.

alternatives.text: Decreased metabolic rate to reduce oxygen demand

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Metabolic rate actually increases at altitude. The primary compensation is increased red cell production via erythropoietin to enhance oxygen-carrying capacity.

params.text:
Clinical Progression

The trekker descends to 3,000 metres and is treated with supplemental oxygen. His symptoms improve. He decides to attempt the altitude again after a week of gradual acclimatisation. During exercise at altitude, his cardiac output increases significantly.

Concept: During exercise, cardiac output increases by increasing both heart rate and stroke volume. Core temperature is maintained at approximately 37°C through a balance of heat production and heat loss.

branchingQuestion.question: During exercise at altitude, how does the cardiovascular system increase cardiac output?

alternatives.text: By increasing both heart rate and stroke volume

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Cardiac output = heart rate x stroke volume. During exercise, both components increase to meet the higher oxygen demand.

alternatives.text: Only by increasing heart rate — stroke volume remains constant during exercise

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Both heart rate AND stroke volume increase during exercise. Stroke volume increases due to increased venous return (Frank-Starling mechanism) and increased contractility.

alternatives.text: By peripheral vasodilation alone without changes in cardiac parameters

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Peripheral vasodilation occurs in exercising muscles, but the primary increase in cardiac output comes from increased heart rate and stroke volume.

params.text:
Clinical Resolution

After gradual acclimatisation over a week, the trekker successfully reaches the summit. His SpO2 has improved to 88% at the same altitude.

Learning points:

  • Altitude acclimatisation: increased EPO leading to polycythaemia
  • Exercise: cardiac output increases via heart rate + stroke volume
  • Core temperature maintained at ~37°C by balancing heat production and loss
  • In starvation, the brain adapts to use ketone bodies as fuel
  • Fever: pyrogens raise hypothalamic set point
